articles.title: A Tiny Claw in a Very Old Fossil Changes Spider History
articles.summary: Scientists found a tiny claw in a 500-million-year-old fossil, and it tells us something amazing about spiders. The fossil is from a sea creature that lived long ago in Utah. At first, it looked like other old fossils, but when a scientist named Rudy cleaned it, he saw a claw where an antenna should be. This claw is called a chelicera, and it's special because it's the oldest one ever found. Chelicerates are a group of animals that includes spiders, scorpions, and horseshoe crabs. This discovery shows that spiders and their relatives started to look like they do today much earlier than we thought, about 20 million years earlier. The fossil helps us understand how these animals evolved from ancient sea creatures. It's like finding a missing piece in a puzzle about life on Earth.

articles.title: Rocks That Look Like Elephant Skin Show Old Ocean Life
articles.summary: A scientist named Rowan Martindale found a rock in Morocco that had wrinkles like elephant skin. She thought it looked like fossilized microbial mats, which are patterns made by tiny living things called microbes. These microbes lived over 180 million years ago in the deep ocean. Usually, scientists think such wrinkles form in shallow water where microbes use sunlight. But this rock was from deep water, where it's dark. Martindale and her team have a new idea: an underwater landslide brought food to the seafloor, helping microbes grow without sunlight. They used chemicals for energy, like some microbes do today on whale carcasses in the deep ocean. This discovery helps us learn about ancient life in dark places.

articles.title: SpaceX Starship: A Faster Trip to Uranus
articles.summary: Uranus is a planet far away in our solar system that scientists want to explore more. It has strange features like spinning on its side and icy moons. The only spacecraft to visit was Voyager 2, which flew by 40 years ago. Now, SpaceX is building a new rocket called Starship that could make trips to Uranus much faster. Starship is big and powerful, and it might be able to refuel in space, helping it travel quicker. Scientists at MIT studied how Starship could help a mission to Uranus by acting like a heat shield to slow down and enter orbit. This could cut travel time in half, making it easier and cheaper to explore Uranus in the 2030s.

articles.title: Making French Fries Healthier with Microwaves
articles.summary: French fries are tasty but can be unhealthy because they soak up a lot of oil when fried. Scientists at the University of Illinois are trying to make fries healthier by using microwaves during frying. They found that microwaves can help reduce the oil that gets into the fries while keeping them crispy. This method might also make cooking faster. The researchers used a special microwave fryer and tested potatoes to see how much oil they absorbed. They explained that when food cooks, water inside turns to steam and pushes oil out, but sometimes oil gets sucked in. Microwaves help by creating more steam inside the fries, which keeps the oil out. This could help people enjoy fries with less fat and fewer calories.

articles.title: How Octopuses Find a Mate
articles.summary: Octopuses are amazing sea animals that can change color and have arms that can think for themselves. Scientists studied how octopuses mate, which is tricky because they live alone in the deep ocean. In an experiment, they put a male and female octopus in a tank with a barrier that had holes. The male used a special arm called a hectocotylus to reach through a hole and touch the female. He then inserted it into her body to deliver sperm, and both stayed still for about an hour. The scientists found that only females give a chemical signal that tells the male it's okay to mate. This helps the male find the right spot inside the female without seeing it. The study shows how octopuses use hormones to guide their mating, making it a unique and careful process.

articles.title: Why Artemis II Isn't Landing on the Moon
articles.summary: NASA is sending a big rocket called Artemis II to the Moon with astronauts for the first time in over 50 years. But this mission won't land on the Moon. Instead, it's a practice trip to get ready for a future landing. The goal is to help astronauts learn how to travel to the Moon safely and explore space further. The rocket is launching from Florida, and people are watching it live. This mission is part of a bigger plan to send humans back to the Moon and maybe even to Mars one day. It's exciting because it helps us learn more about space and prepares for bigger adventures.
